analogy: Think of "Gender Religion and Caste" through the chapter's own opening images: 46 D e m o c r a t i c P o l i t i c s Chapter 4 Overview In this tour of democracy, we have come across political parties several times. In Class IX, we noticed the role of political parties... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 1 Parties contest elections. In most, 2 Parties put forward different, 3 Parties play a decisive role, 4 Parties form and run governments.. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Gender Religion and Caste is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 46 D e m o c r a t i c P o l i t i c s Chapter 4 Overview In this tour of democracy, we have come across political parties several times.

The text explains that in Class IX, we noticed the role of political parties in the rise of democracies, in the formation of constitutional designs, in electoral politics and in the making and working of governments.

The text explains that in this textbook, we have glanced at political parties as vehicles of federal sharing of political power and as negotiators of social divisions in the arena of democratic politics.

The text explains that before concluding this tour, let us take a close look at the nature and working of political parties, especially in our country.

The text explains that we begin by asking two common questions: Why do we need parties?

The text explains that in the light of these, we introduce the national and regional political parties in today’s India and then look at what is wrong with political parties and what can be done about it.

The text explains that p o l i t i c a l P a r t i e s 47 Why do we need political parties?

The text explains that political parties are easily one of the most visible institutions in a democracy.

The text explains that for most ordinary citizens, democracy is equal to political parties.

The text explains that if you travel to remote parts of our country and speak to the less educated citizens, you could come across people who may not know anything about our Constitution or about the nature of our government.

The text explains that but chances are that they would know something about our political parties.

The text explains that at the same time, this visibility does not mean popularity.

The text explains that most people tend to be very critical of political parties.

The text explains that they tend to blame parties for all that is wrong with our democracy and our political life.

The text explains that parties have become identified with social and political divisions.

The text explains that therefore, it is natural to ask – do we need political parties at all?

The text explains that meaning A political party is a group of people who come together to contest elections and hold power in the government.

The text explains that they agree on some policies and programmes for the society with a view to promote the collective good.

The text explains that thus, parties reflect fundamental political divisions in a society.

The text explains that parties are about a part of the society and thus, involve partisanship.

The text explains that each of us may have different opinions and views on what policies are suitable for the society.

The text explains that but no government can handle such a large variety of views.

The text explains that in a democracy, a large number of similar opinions have to be grouped together to provide a direction in which policies can be formulated by the governments.

The text explains that a government is expected to base its policies on the line taken by the ruling party.

The text explains that but since most of the members belong to a party, they go by the direction of the party leadership, irrespective of their personal opinions.

The text explains that parties recruit leaders, train them and then make them ministers to run the government in the way they want.

The text explains that 5 Those parties that lose in the elections play the role of opposition to the parties in power, by voicing different views and criticising government for its failures or wrong policies.

The text explains that opposition parties also mobilise opposition to the government.

The text explains that many of the pressure groups are the extensions of political parties among different sections of society.

The text explains that often opinions in the society crystallise on the lines parties take.

The text explains that 7 Parties provide people access to government machinery and welfare schemes implemented by governments.

The text explains that for an ordinary citizen it is easy to approach a local party leader than a government officer.

The text explains that necessity This list of functions in a sense answers the question asked above: we need political parties because they perform all these functions.

The text explains that the government may be formed, but its utility will remain ever uncertain.

The text explains that that is the reason we find political parties in almost all countries of the world, whether these countries are big or small, old or new, developed or developing.

The text explains that the rise of political parties is directly linked to the emergence of representative democracies.

The text explains that ruling Party: Political party that runs government.

The text explains that as societies became large and complex, they also needed some agency to gather different views on various issues and to present these to the government.

The text explains that find one photograph or news clipping from your own area for each of the functions listed above. responsible government could be formed.

The text explains that they needed a mechanism to support or restrain the government, make policies, justify or oppose them.

The text explains that political parties fulfill these needs that every representative government has.

The text explains that usually only a handful of parties are effectively in the race to win elections and form the government.

The text explains that so the question is: how many major or effective parties are good for a democracy?

The text explains that in some countries, only one party is allowed to control and run the government.

The text explains that 3: Activists of CPI (M), CPI, OGP and JD (S) take out a rally in Bhubaneswar to protest against POSCO, the Korean steel company for being permitted by the State Government to export iron ore from Orissa to feed steel plants in China and Korea. © (1) C V Subrahmanyan (2) K Gopinathan (3) A Chakrabarty, The Hindu 1 3 2

The text explains that although, legally speaking, people are free to form political parties, it does not happen because the electoral system does not permit free competition for power.

The text explains that we cannot consider one-party system as a good option because this is not a democratic option.

The text explains that any democratic system must allow at least two parties to compete in elections and provide a fair chance for the competing parties to come to power.

The text explains that his objective was to foster proper leadership, good governance and build a new Bangladesh.

The text explains that he felt that only a political party different from the traditional ones would bring about new political culture.

The text explains that his party would be democratic from the grassroots level.

The text explains that the launching of the new party, called Nagarik Shakti (Citizens’ Power), has caused a stir among the Bangladeshis.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Democratic Politics II, Chapter 4: Gender Religion and Caste
